core_meaning: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> refers to a tangible thing or object, but it also works as a nominalizer and softener in speech — turning verbs or adjectives into noun-like concepts (「<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるものがない」= 'there's nothing to eat') and expressing general truths, expectations, or emotional nuance (「<ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>というものは…」= 'children, as a general thing...'). It sits somewhere between a concrete word and a grammatical tool.

sentences.jp: <ruby>冷蔵庫<rt>れいぞうこ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるものが<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>もない。
sentences.translation: There's nothing to eat in the fridge.
sentences.notes: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> nominalizes「<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる」to mean 'something to eat' — a very natural everyday use.

sentences.jp: これ、<ruby>大事<rt>たいじ</rt></ruby>なものだから<ruby>失<rt>なく</rt></ruby>くさないでね。
sentences.translation: This is something important, so don't lose it, okay?
sentences.notes: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> used simply as 'thing' or 'object,' softened with「〜からね」for a gentle reminder.

sentences.jp: <ruby>人間<rt>にんげん</rt></ruby>というものは、なかなか<ruby>変<rt>か</rt></ruby>われないものだ。
sentences.translation: People, as a rule, don't change very easily.
sentences.notes: 「〜というものは〜ものだ」expresses a general truth or observation about the nature of something — one of <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>'s most important grammatical roles.

sentences.jp: <ruby>昔<rt>むかし</rt></ruby>は<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby>ここで<ruby>遊<rt>あそ</rt></ruby>んだものです。
sentences.translation: I used to play here every day when I was young.
sentences.notes: 「〜たものだ」expresses nostalgic recollection — a set grammatical pattern where <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> carries an emotional, reflective nuance.

sentences.jp: <ruby>良<rt>よ</rt></ruby>い<ruby>文章<rt>ぶんしょう</rt></ruby>というものは、<ruby>読<rt>よ</rt></ruby>む<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>の<ruby>心<rt>こころ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>残<rt>のこ</rt></ruby>るものでなければならない。
sentences.translation: Good writing is something that must leave a lasting impression on the reader.
sentences.notes: 「〜というものは〜ものでなければならない」is a formal written pattern stating what something ought to be by its very nature.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> for abstract, intangible concepts — use <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> instead. For example, 「<ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>なもの」can work for a treasured object, but 「<ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>なこと」is needed when you mean an important matter or idea. Also avoid using <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> for people — 「<ruby>大事<rt>たいじ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>」not 「<ruby>大事<rt>たいじ</rt></ruby>なもの」when referring to a person.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying 「<ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しいものを<ruby>説明<rt>せつめい</rt></ruby>してください」when you mean 'please explain something difficult (as a topic)' — here <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> is more natural because you're referring to an abstract matter, not a physical thing. Use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> when the referent is concrete or tangible (or a grammatical softener/generalizer); use <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> for facts, actions, and abstract ideas. Compare: 「<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるもの」(something to eat — physical) vs. 「<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>っていること」(something one knows — abstract fact).

frequency: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers — daily conversation, literature, formal writing. Its grammatical uses (「〜ものだ」「〜たものだ」「〜というものは」) appear frequently in both spoken and written Japanese and are important for N3 and beyond.
anki_hint: <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> = tangible things + a softener/generalizer: if you can touch it or you're making a general statement about 'the nature of something,' reach for <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>; if it's an abstract fact or action, reach for <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby>.
